fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multiple independent CPU cores on the same chip. A core, for this purpose, is the bundle of front-end, back-end, Execution unitss, and L1 Cache hierarchy that runs one architectural thread of execution.

Traditional multiprocessor systems put each core on its own physical chip; chip multiprocessing (CMP) puts multiple cores on the same die. The key advantage is shared lower-level caches — typically an L2 or L3 shared across all cores on the chip — which speeds up inter-core communication and amortizes silicon.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The shift to multicore was forced by the Power wall more than chosen for its own merits. The software cost is real: shared-memory parallelism requires correctness work that single-threaded code didn't.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multicore is what makes Concurrency a hardware problem, not just a scheduling problem: real simultaneous execution on different physical cores means software has to handle Race conditions with real synchronization primitives like Locks.
Notes on the book Inside the Machine by Jon Stokes, restructured as a concept hierarchy.

The CPU's nearest tier of storage — the first NUMA level. Registers sit physically next to the ALUs and are addressed by name in the instruction encoding rather than by memory address. Their count is limited by the chip's surface area, which is why main memory exists at all.

The register file is the structure that holds the architecturally-visible registers. To support a superscalar machine, the file needs enough read and write ports to feed every ALU simultaneously: for an n-ALU chip with two operands per op, that's 2n read ports and n write ports.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Die area for a register file scales quadratically in the number of ports.
Solution. Split it. Different execution units (integer, floating-point, vector) get their own smaller register files. Fewer ports per file, faster access, less area.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Beyond the architecturally-named registers, modern chips keep many extra rename registers used by register renaming to break false data dependencies.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Instructions distinguish "immediate types" from "register types" so the decoder knows whether a field encodes a register name or a literal value.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The classical model of CPU operation: each instruction goes through three phases.

html:ol.html:li: Fetch: read the instruction from memory into the instruction register; increment the program counter.
Decode: extract the opcode, the operand register names, and the destination from the instruction register.
Execute: route to the appropriate functional unit — ALU for arithmetic, load/store unit for memory ops, branch unit for branches.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Instructions don't take arbitrary time. The motherboard's clock cycle bounds when latched values become visible to the next stage.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: pipeline decomposes this loop into independently-running pipeline stages so multiple instructions are in flight at once. Superscalar machines run multiple instructions per stage. Both are extensions of this same three-phase model.
An instruction that potentially changes the program counter to something other than "the next sequential instruction." Two flavors:

html:ol.html:li: Unconditional: the PC is set to the destination address, period.
Conditional: the PC is set to the destination only if some condition holds.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Conditional branches require the hardware to remember the outcome of recent arithmetic — typically in a processor status word (PSW) register, with one bit per condition (zero, sign, carry, overflow). Each conditional-branch variant tests a particular PSW bit.

In a pipelined CPU, branches stall the front-end: until the branch resolves, the fetcher doesn't know what to fetch next. This is the control hazard, and modern machines mitigate it with Speculative execution driven by Branch prediction.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. If the program uses relative addressing, the OS supplies a code-segment base to add to the branch destination.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The fixed series of steps a machine performs from power-on until a usable operating system is running. Each stage runs from a known location and hands off control to the next.

html:ol.html:li: Power signal resets the CPU to a known state.
The CPU fetches from a hard-wired address — the BIOS (or UEFI firmware). The BIOS runs basic hardware checks (POST).
The BIOS loads and jumps to the bootloader (e.g. GRUB) from a known location on disk.
The bootloader places the kernel image at the correct address and jumps to its entry point.
The kernel performs early setup: installs the Trap table (a special privileged instruction), starts the timer interrupt, sets up memory management, and finally enters user space.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The hardware is hard-wired only to find the BIOS. Everything past that is software the user can replace, which is why dual-booting and custom bootloaders work.

fr:title.text: Pipelining overlaps instruction stages to retire one per cycle

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Pipelining overlaps instruction stages to retire one per cycle

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Decomposing the Fetch-execute loop into independently-running stages so that multiple instructions are in flight simultaneously. A classical four-stage pipeline:

html:ol.html:li: Fetch from memory.
Decode.
Execute (the original execute phase, minus the write).
Write the result back to register or memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each stage runs concurrently on different instructions. The clock cycle is bounded by the slowest stage, not the whole loop — so dividing the work into roughly-equal-length stages is essential. With n stages running in steady state, the machine retires one instruction per cycle even though each instruction takes n cycles to traverse.

html:ol.html:li: Completion rate: instructions completed per nanosecond. Program time is \frac {\text {instructions per program}}{\text {avg completion rate}}.
Instruction throughput: instructions retired per clock cycle.
Instruction latency: cycles to complete one instruction (\geq the number of stages, often more in practice).

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Pipelining doesn't make any one instruction faster — it raises throughput. Pushing this further (deeper pipelines, more stages) shrinks the clock cycle but increases per-instruction latency and the cost of pipeline stalls or flushes.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: What stops the steady-state throughput of one instruction per cycle is hazards: data hazard, structural hazard, and control hazard. The next layer of complication is going superscalar — multiple instructions per stage — which is covered in superscalar.

fr:title.text: Superscalar CPUs issue multiple instructions per cycle

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Superscalar CPUs issue multiple instructions per cycle

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multiple Execution unitss on the same chip running in parallel during the same pipeline stage. Decode becomes decode/dispatch: the front-end must decide which instructions can issue in parallel and route them to the right units. Going superscalar amplifies the cost of every pipeline data hazard and control hazard, which is why the rest of this chapter's machinery — Forwarding, register renaming, Branch prediction — exists.
The functional circuitry that actually performs an instruction. A superscalar CPU contains several, dispatched to in parallel during the execute stage of the pipeline. The common families:

html:ul.html:li: ALU — arithmetic logic unit, performs arithmetic (add, sub, mul, div) and logical (AND, OR, NOT) operations on integers and on special registers like the PSW. Typically split into integer logic units (ILU) and floating-point units (FPU).
Load-store unit — performs memory loads and stores. Carries a small address-calculation adder for address-mode resolution.
Branch execution unit — evaluates branch conditions (often by reading the PSW) and signals whether the branch is taken.
Vector / SIMD units — operate on wide registers (SSE, AVX, AltiVec) for data-parallel work.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Per the convention in Inside the Machine, "ALU" often refers to any execution unit for arithmetic or logical operations — including integer logic units (ILU) and floating-point units (FPU).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each unit's reads and writes flow through a Register file. Splitting the register file per-unit reduces port count and access time.

The internal organization of a CPU that implements a given instruction set architecture (ISA). Two chips can present identical ISAs to software while having radically different microarchitectures — e.g. Intel and AMD both implement x86, but their pipelines, branch predictors, and execution units differ.

The bridge between the two is the microcode engine: a mini-CPU inside the CPU. It holds small programs in a microcode ROM and runs them when a complex instruction needs to be translated into the simpler primitives the hardware actually executes. Conceptually, it's an emulator.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. CISC vs RISC-style ISAs need large microcode engines to implement their complex instructions. RISC designs minimize the microcode engine, pushing translation to the compiler instead.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The translated primitives are Micro-operations — a finer-grained, more uniform instruction stream that the actual hardware schedules. The translation step is the main reason an x86 chip's pipeline starts with such complex decode logic.

fr:title.text: Data hazards stall when an instruction needs a not-yet-written value

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Data hazards stall when an instruction needs a not-yet-written value

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When one instruction's result is the next instruction's input, a naive pipeline would have to stall: the second instruction can't execute until the first has written back. Data hazards are the most pervasive source of stalls in a CPU.
Two standard mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Forwarding — route the producer's output directly into the consumer's input, before the writeback stage commits it. The consumer sees the result one cycle earlier.

register renaming — false dependencies, where two instructions write to the same architectural register but operate on independent values, can be broken by mapping the architectural register to two different physical registers.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A data hazard mitigation: the producing instruction's result is routed from the output of the execute stage directly to the input of the next instruction's execute stage, bypassing the register-file write and read. The dependent consumer can execute one cycle later instead of waiting for writeback.
Conceptually, the pipeline grows wiring rather than stages. The result is "forwarded" from where it appears to where it's needed.

Register renaming is the data hazard mitigation that makes out-of-order execution viable: the hardware dynamically remaps each architectural-register write to a fresh physical register from a larger pool, so two instructions that reuse the same architectural name for independent values never collide. The false data dependency — same architectural register, unrelated values — simply disappears, because each write lands in a different physical register.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. Consider: add r1, r2, r3 // r2 := r1 + r3 add r4, r5, r2 // r2 := r4 + r5 (reuses r2 for an independent value) The second add only depends on r2 as a destination, not as a source. Without renaming, it would have to wait for the first instruction's writeback to avoid clobbering. With renaming, the second write goes to a fresh physical register, and the two instructions can proceed in parallel.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The programming model exposes only the architecturally-named registers; the Register file hides a larger pool of rename registers underneath. This is one of the central tricks that makes out-of-order execution viable.

fr:title.text: What false dependencies does register renaming eliminate?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What false dependencies does register renaming eliminate?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Write-after-write (\mathrm {WAW}) and write-after-read (\mathrm {WAR}) hazards — the false dependencies that arise from reusing an architectural register, not from real data flow. Removing them exposes more Register renaming breaks false dependencies to the scheduler.

fr:title.text: Structural hazards stall when instructions contend for one resource

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Structural hazards stall when instructions contend for one resource

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two simultaneous instructions need the same hardware resource, and there's only one of it. The classic example is a single-port Register file facing two writes in one cycle, or two memory-accessing instructions facing a single load-store unit.
Mitigations cost area:

html:ul.html:li: Duplicate the resource (extra ALU, more register-file write ports).
Pipeline the resource (multi-cycle dividers that accept a new input each cycle).
Stall — accept the cost in throughput.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Register file area scales quadratically in port count, so duplicating ports for many ALUs gets expensive quickly.
Solution. Per-unit register files (one for integer, one for FP, one for vector) with fewer ports each. Discussed under Register file.

fr:title.text: Control hazards stall fetch until the branch resolves

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Control hazards stall fetch until the branch resolves

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When the CPU reaches a Branch instruction in the pipeline, it doesn't yet know what to fetch next. Until the branch's condition resolves and its target is computed — several stages later — fetch is stuck.
Mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Branch prediction — guess where control flow is going.
Speculative execution — actually run instructions down the predicted path, ready to throw the work away if the guess was wrong.
Instruction caching — keep recently-fetched instructions handy so the cost of restarting fetch on a mispredict is at least bounded by cache latency, not DRAM latency.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The cost of a mispredicted branch is roughly the pipeline depth — every speculatively-issued instruction has to be squashed and the pipeline refilled. Deeper pipelines amplify this cost, which is one reason Pentium 4 vs G4e: design philosophies (deep pipeline, narrow & deep) and superscalar-style designs (wider, shallower) make different tradeoffs.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The mechanism that lets the front-end of a pipelined CPU keep fetching past a Branch instruction before the branch has actually resolved. The fetcher consults a predictor; if the prediction is right, the pipeline runs at full throughput; if wrong, the speculatively-issued instructions are squashed.

Program-agnostic rules: e.g. "always predict taken", "always predict not-taken", or "predict backward branches taken, forward branches not-taken". Cheap, but accuracy is poor outside of loop-heavy code.
Driven by per-branch history kept in two hardware tables:

html:ul.html:li: Branch history table (BHT) — indexed by branch address, holds a small counter (typically 2-bit saturating) tracking how often the branch was taken.
Branch target buffer (BTB) — holds the target address each branch was observed to jump to. A BTB miss is expensive: even if you correctly predict "taken", you still don't know where to fetch from.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Per-branch prediction allows a counter to learn pattern-specific behavior — e.g. a loop counter takes the back-edge N - 1 times then falls through.

The Pentium famously combined the two: static prediction on a BHT miss, dynamic prediction otherwise. Later machines added refinements — PowerPC 750 added a Trace cache-like branch target instruction cache (BTIC) that stores the first few instructions at the target, eliminating the re-fetch latency. The Pentium M added a loop detector for loops that always iterate the same number of times.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. See Speculative execution for what happens once a prediction has been made.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Run instructions before knowing whether they should actually be run, on the assumption that you'll usually be right. Used after Branch prediction to keep the pipeline busy past a Branch instruction: instructions on the predicted path enter the pipeline, execute, and stash their results in non-architectural state. Only when the branch resolves are speculated results "committed" to architectural state — or, if the prediction was wrong, discarded.
Speculative state lives in the Reorder buffer (and the register renaming rename registers). Entries are tagged as speculative; on misprediction the speculative entries are flushed.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Modern speculative execution drives a lot of CPU complexity — and a lot of CPU security work. Spectre-class side channels exploit the fact that speculative execution can leave traces in caches even after the speculative results are discarded.

fr:title.text: Out-of-order execution runs ready instructions past a stalled one, committing in program order

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Out-of-order execution runs ready instructions past a stalled one, committing in program order

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Execute instructions in whatever order their operands become available, then commit them back in program order. The programmer's model still sees sequential execution; the hardware reorders aggressively underneath to keep Execution units busy across data hazards and memory latency.
Two structures make this work:

html:ul.html:li: Reservation stations — hold instructions waiting on operands, so a stalled instruction doesn't block independent ones behind it.
Reorder buffer — preserves program order so commits look sequential and mispredicted Speculative execution paths can be unwound.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Combined with register renaming to break false dependencies, the back-end of a modern CPU is essentially a dynamic scheduler that uses the program order only as a hint about what to retire next.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. An out-of-order pipeline can issue a load early (because its address operands are ready), execute several unrelated ALU ops while waiting for the memory, then have all four instructions ready to commit in program order. The illusion of sequential execution is preserved; the parallelism is real.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The structure that lets an out-of-order execution CPU look in-order to the programmer. The ROB holds every in-flight instruction in original program order, regardless of the order they actually executed. When the oldest entry has its result ready and all earlier entries have already committed, the ROB commits it — writing its result to architectural state and freeing the slot.

For stores, commit is only half the story. The ROB entry is freed and the instruction is architecturally real, but the value has not yet reached the cache — it is handed off to the Store buffer for asynchronous drain. This is what keeps the ROB from stalling behind every cache miss: once a store is known to be non-speculative, the front-end can move on.
Each ROB entry typically tracks:

html:ul.html:li: Instruction identity and program order position.
Operand register names (and renamed sources).
Status — pending, executing, complete, speculated.
The instruction's result, held in a data field that doubles as a register renaming register.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Speculatively-issued instructions also enter the ROB but are tagged. On a branch misprediction, all speculative entries past the mispredicted branch are squashed.

The Pentium Pro introduced this design with a 40-entry ROB. The ROB sits at both ends of the pipeline — dispatch enters it, retirement leaves it — which is what makes the rest of the back-end free to issue and execute out of order.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. ROB size, Reservation station count, and pipeline depth set each other's bounds. A shallow pipeline can afford a small ROB; a deep one (Pentium 4 had 126) needs a large one to keep the back-end fed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A buffer in front of an Execution units (or group of them) that holds dispatched instructions waiting on their operands. Once all operands arrive — either by writeback or by Forwarding — the instruction is issued to its execution unit. Reservation stations exist so that an instruction waiting on a slow operand doesn't block dispatch of unrelated instructions behind it.
Two organizational choices:

html:ul.html:li: One reservation station per execution unit (Pentium Pro). Smaller, simpler.
One shared station feeding multiple units (also seen on Pentium Pro, where it issues up to five micro-ops per cycle).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Within a single station, instructions issue in FIFO order. Across stations, there is no guarantee — that's what out-of-order execution actually means.
Dispatching an instruction into a reservation station has four prerequisites:

html:ol.html:li: In-order rule: instructions dispatch in program order (they execute out of order, not dispatch).
Execution unit availability: the matching reservation station has room.
Completion buffer availability: the Reorder buffer has room to track the new in-flight instruction.
Register-rename availability: enough rename registers exist to hold the new instruction's destination.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The mechanism by which a modern out-of-order CPU notices that a previously-stalled instruction now has all its operands ready. The issue queue holds the N in-flight instructions whose operands are not yet available; each entry watches a small set of physical register tags (the operands it is waiting for). When a producer finishes, it broadcasts "tag T is ready" to every entry, and each entry compares that tag against its own waiting tags through a content-addressable memory (CAM). Entries whose last operand arrives become eligible to issue this cycle; a separate select stage picks which of the now-ready entries actually fires.

The cost is the reason CPU issue queues are small. Each entry runs a CAM lookup against each broadcast tag, so an N-entry queue with N producers per cycle scales as O(N^2) in comparators, wires, and power. The select stage compounds this — priority encoders across N entries are themselves a quadratic-area structure on the critical timing path. The cumulative wakeup-select latency is what bounds clock frequency once N grows past a few tens.

This is the structural reason CPU out-of-order windows stay in the tens-to-low-hundreds of entries even as transistor budgets grow. Wider doesn't help: timing closure on the CAM degrades faster than the additional ILP repays. GPUs sidestep the whole problem by leaning on Thread-level parallelism — a cheap per-register scoreboard plus a warp scheduler covers stalls without any broadcast/compare structure.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Wakeup-select sits at the boundary between Reservation station (which holds the instruction) and register renaming (which named the tags being broadcast). All three structures together are what people mean by "the out-of-order back-end."

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two design philosophies for an instruction set architecture.

html:ul.html:li: Complex instruction set computing (CISC) — many specialized instructions, including ones that combine memory access with arithmetic or operate on complex types (strings, decimal numbers). One instruction often does the work of several. x86 is the canonical example.

Reduced instruction set computing (RISC) — a small set of simple, uniform instructions; explicit load and store; arithmetic only on registers. Each instruction does little but maps cleanly to hardware. PowerPC, MIPS, ARM are RISC.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. CISC needs a fat Microarchitecture engine to translate its complex instructions into hardware-executable primitives. That's silicon area, power, and design complexity that doesn't directly add throughput.

Solution. Once RISC clearly won as a hardware model, x86 vendors adopted instruction translation: a CISC front-end decodes x86 into RISC-like Micro-operations internally. The chip is RISC on the inside, CISC on the outside.

Remark. The cost of legacy is real. The original Pentium spent roughly 30% of its transistors on legacy support (mostly the microcode ROM). The Pentium Pro spent 40% on backward compatibility. Modern x86 chips have absorbed this overhead because they can — transistor budgets grew faster than the legacy tax.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The hardware-internal instruction stream that an x86 chip actually schedules. Each user-visible x86 instruction is decoded into one or more micro-ops, which are simpler, more uniform, RISC-like primitives that the back-end can execute, reorder, and rename freely.

Many common x86 instructions translate to just one or two micro-ops, so decoders are split into simple and complex variants — the simple decoders are specific to single-micro-op instructions and run in parallel with the complex one.
Optimizations that operate on the micro-op stream:

html:ul.html:li: Micro-op fusion (Pentium M) — two related micro-ops that the back-end tracks together while sharing a slot in the issue queue. A store decomposes into a store-address and store-data pair, which are fused; a read-modify decomposes into a load + ALU op that fuse and execute serially.

Macro fusion (Core 2 Duo) — fuse a pair of x86 instructions (e.g. compare + branch) before decode, so a single micro-op represents both. Increases peak decode bandwidth without growing ROB or RS pressure.
Trace cache (Pentium 4) — cache decoded micro-op sequences for hot code so decoding doesn't have to happen on every iteration.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Micro-ops exist because CISC vs RISC-style ISAs need to be translated to something the hardware can schedule cleanly. Pure RISC chips don't need them.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An L1-level cache of decoded Micro-operations sequences. Instead of caching raw x86 instructions that have to be decoded on every fetch, a trace cache stores micro-op traces ready for the back-end. Introduced in the Pentium 4 to keep its very deep pipeline fed when normal x86 decoding couldn't run fast enough.
The trace cache operates in two modes:

html:ul.html:li: Execution mode: on a trace hit, feed the cached micro-ops directly to the back-end. Decode is bypassed entirely.
Build mode: on a trace miss, fetch the instructions from L2, decode them, and assemble a new trace while feeding it forward.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The trace cache is indexed during Branch prediction, so speculative paths beyond a branch can be stored in the same trace and fed forward without restarting fetch. This lets a single cache line hold six micro-ops that may include a speculated branch target.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Long x86 instructions that decode to hundreds of micro-ops would blow out the trace cache.
Solution. Tag those instructions. When the trace cache encounters a tag, it stores a pointer to the microcode ROM rather than the micro-ops themselves, and the ROM streams them on demand.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The CPU clock is decreasing much faster than main-memory latency, so without a cache hierarchy a fast CPU spends most of its time stalled on memory.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. If you are a really fast factory, the size of the truck doesn't matter — you must still wait the full duration of the first delivery.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multiple tiers of progressively-larger, progressively-slower storage between the CPU's registers and main memory. Each tier holds the most-recently-used contents of the next slower tier, exploiting Spatial locality and Temporal locality in the workload.
Approximate tiers on a modern x86 chip:

html:ul.html:li: Registers — ns, dozens of bytes per core.
L1 cache — 2–8 ns, 8–128 KB per core. Often split into instruction and data L1s.
Built from static RAM (4–6 transistors per cell), which is fast but area-expensive vs. DRAM's single-transistor cell.
L2 cache — 5–12 ns, 0.5–8 MB. Closes the latency gap between L1 and DRAM.
L3 cache — slower, shared across cores.
Main memory — 10–60 ns, GBs.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. A byte's journey. L1 checked first; on miss, L2; on miss, main memory; on miss in main memory, Page fault to disk.

Remark. L1 is split into instruction and data caches because the locality profiles of code and data are very different — re-running the same code over different data has high temporal locality for code but low for data.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A cache miss happens when the requested data isn't in cache and has to be fetched from a slower tier. Misses fall into four categories — the "four Cs" of cache misses:

html:ol.html:li: Compulsory miss — the data was never in cache. Cold-start misses are unavoidable for a given cache size and working set.
Conflict miss — the data was evicted because of an associativity collision. A different access mapped to the same set kicked it out. Reduced by higher Cache associativity.
Coherence miss — the data was invalidated because another core wrote to it. Specific to shared-memory multicore systems.
Capacity miss — the cache filled up and the data was evicted by Cache replacement policy. The working set doesn't fit.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Distinguishing them matters because they have different mitigations: conflict misses respond to associativity changes, capacity misses respond to size, coherence misses respond to cache-coherence protocol design, and compulsory misses respond to prefetching.

The empirical observation that if a program accesses memory address x, it is likely to access addresses near x soon. Sequential array traversal, struct field access, and instruction fetch all exhibit it.

Caches exploit spatial locality by loading whole cache lines (typically 64 bytes) on every miss, not just the single requested byte. The byte that triggered the load is the critical word; the surrounding bytes come along for free under the assumption you'll want them.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. RAM and caches are organized in cache-line granularity throughout. Bandwidth is sized for whole-line transfers; tags are per-line, not per-byte.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The complement is Temporal locality — repeated access to the same data over time. Temporal locality implies spatial locality (you re-touch nearby bytes when you re-touch the original), but not vice versa.
If a program accesses memory address x, it is likely to access x again soon. Loop variables, frequently-called functions, and re-used data structures all exhibit it.

Caches exploit temporal locality by retaining recently-accessed lines until something more important takes their slot. The Cache replacement policy is what decides "more important" — typically LRU and the clock algorithm-ish.
Temporal locality implies spatial locality (re-touching a byte tends to re-touch its neighbors) but not vice versa: a sequential scan has spatial locality with no temporal locality.
The rule that decides which slots in the cache an address is allowed to occupy. Three points on the spectrum:

html:ul.html:li: Direct mapped — every address maps to exactly one slot. Fast (only one tag to check) but suffers conflict misses: two hot addresses that map to the same slot evict each other.
Fully associative — any address can live in any slot. No conflict misses but tag check is expensive (compare against every entry in parallel).

n-way set associative — middle ground. The cache is divided into sets of n slots; an address maps to a specific set but can occupy any of its n slots. The hot setting on real caches is somewhere between 4-way and 16-way.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Higher associativity reduces conflict misses but increases tag-check hardware and power. The cache tag of an address is the bits that aren't used to pick the set — they're stored alongside the data and compared on every access.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The tag array is stored separately from the data array (tag RAM) so the tag comparison can happen in parallel with the data lookup. Tags are small, allowing many in-flight comparisons.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Within a Cache associativity set, when a new line arrives and the set is full, which existing line gets evicted? Same question as LRU and the clock algorithm for pages, different timescales.

The hot policy is least recently used — but pure LRU requires tracking a per-line ordering and updating it on every hit, which is too expensive at L1 latencies. Real caches use pseudo-LRU — a tree of single bits per set that approximates LRU with a fraction of the bookkeeping. The clock algorithm is one well-known pseudo-LRU scheme.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Block (cache line) size is part of the same tradeoff. Larger blocks improve Spatial locality but cause cache pollution (you fetch bytes you don't use) and eat memory bandwidth. The sweet spot is around 64 bytes on modern x86.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When a cached line is modified, when do you propagate the change to the next tier?

html:ul.html:li: Write-through — every store updates both the cache and main memory. Simple, but generates memory traffic on every write.

Write-back — stores update only the cache. The line is marked dirty via a per-line bit; the next-tier copy is updated only when the line is evicted. Less memory traffic but the cache is now the sole authoritative source until eviction, which complicates correctness.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Real CPUs run write-back at L1 and L2. Write-through is occasionally used for specific lines (e.g. memory-mapped device registers where every write must hit the device immediately).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Write-back is one of the constraints behind cache-coherence protocols on multicore chips: every core must agree on which one currently owns a dirty line.
The hard limit on per-core clock speed that emerged in the early 2000s, ending the era of frequency scaling. Power dissipation has two components on a chip:

html:ul.html:li: Dynamic power density — watts per unit area dissipated by transistor switching. Scales with clock frequency: faster switching, more energy per second. You can also drive it up by cramming more transistors into the same area.
Static power density — watts per unit area dissipated by leakage current through "off" transistors. Smaller transistors leak more, so denser chips leak more even when idle.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By the mid-2000s, single-core frequency scaling was thermally unsustainable. The industry pivoted to low-power design and Multicore — get more throughput from many slower cores instead of one faster one. Pentium M and the Core series were the first generations explicitly designed under this constraint.

Multiple independent CPU cores on the same chip. A core, for this purpose, is the bundle of front-end, back-end, Execution unitss, and L1 Cache hierarchy that runs one architectural thread of execution.

Traditional multiprocessor systems put each core on its own physical chip; chip multiprocessing (CMP) puts multiple cores on the same die. The key advantage is shared lower-level caches — typically an L2 or L3 shared across all cores on the chip — which speeds up inter-core communication and amortizes silicon.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The shift to multicore was forced by the Power wall more than chosen for its own merits. The software cost is real: shared-memory parallelism requires correctness work that single-threaded code didn't.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multicore is what makes Concurrency a hardware problem, not just a scheduling problem: real simultaneous execution on different physical cores means software has to handle Race conditions with real synchronization primitives like Locks.

What the CPU does, entirely in hardware, the moment an interrupt arrives — whether a device IRQ, a fault like a Page fault, or a software int N. The point of the hardware path is to land in the kernel at a clean instruction boundary with enough saved state that user execution can resume later as if nothing happened.
Four things happen, in order, with no software involvement:

html:ol.html:li: Reach a clean instruction boundary. The CPU cannot pause halfway through, say, a memory write — architectural state would be inconsistent. So the current instruction either finishes normally (typical for asynchronous interrupts like a timer) or is rolled back as if it never started (faults, where the instruction couldn't complete anyway and needs to be retried). A page-fault handler maps in the missing page and UHNL returns to re-execute the faulting instruction; a timer handler returns to the next instruction.

Save the minimum resume state to the kernel stack. The CPU pushes RIP (where to resume), RSP (the user stack pointer it's about to abandon), the privilege level (CS — were we in ring 3 or ring 0?), and RFLAGS. The save target is the kernel stack, whose address the CPU reads from the Task state segment that the OS set up at boot. This is a security boundary: the kernel never trusts the user stack, since user code could have corrupted it or pointed RSP at something malicious. Note what is not saved: general-purpose registers. The handler itself pushes RAX, RBX, ... if it uses them, which keeps the hardware path fast.

Jump to the handler via the Interrupt descriptor table. The CPU takes the interrupt number N, indexes entry N of the IDT (found via the IDTR register), loads the handler address into RIP, and loads the new code segment, which switches the CPU from ring 3 to ring 0. After this step, the CPU is running kernel code on the kernel stack at the right privilege level — all set up automatically.

Flush the Reorder buffer of work past the interrupt point. In an out-of-order execution CPU the ROB may already contain instructions from after the interrupt — they've been issued speculatively because the front-end ran ahead. Those entries are discarded so the handler sees clean architectural state, as if execution had stopped exactly at the interrupt point. When UHNL returns, those instructions re-execute normally. This is the same squash machinery used for branch mispredictions in Speculative execution — and, famously, the reason Spectre-class side channels exist: speculative work leaves observable traces in caches even after the ROB is flushed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Once the hardware path finishes, the OS's interrupt handler is running. Its first job is typically to save the remaining registers before doing real work — the rest is software.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Steps 2 and 3 are why an OS needs to set up the IDT and TSS before enabling interrupts. Until both structures point somewhere valid, the first interrupt would either jump to garbage or push to a user-controlled stack — both fatal.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The CPU's lookup table from interrupt number to handler. The OS allocates the IDT in memory, fills entry N with the address of the handler for interrupt N, and points the IDTR register at the table's base. From then on the Hardware interrupt path uses the IDT to dispatch every interrupt without any further software help.

Each entry — a gate descriptor on x86 — carries enough information for the CPU to switch into the handler safely: the handler's linear address, the code segment to load (which sets the new privilege level, typically ring 0), and a type byte distinguishing interrupt gates (which mask further interrupts on entry) from trap gates (which don't). Entries 0–31 are reserved for CPU-defined exceptions (divide-by-zero, Page fault, double fault, ...); 32–255 are available for device IRQs and software interrupts.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The IDT is one of three OS-installed tables that the CPU consults transparently — the others are the GDT (segment descriptors) and the page tables. Each is a hardware contract: the OS lays bytes out in a format the silicon understands, then hands over a pointer.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A small OS-allocated structure that the CPU reads to find the kernel stack pointer when it raises privilege. Its historical purpose — full task switching in hardware — is dead on modern x86-64; what survives is one essential field: RSP0, the address of the kernel stack to switch to on entry from ring 3.

During the Hardware interrupt path, the moment the CPU decides to push RIP/RSP/CS/RFLAGS, it reads RSP0 from the current TSS (located via the GDT entry pointed at by the TR register) and switches RSP to that value before pushing. If the TSS were missing or pointed at user memory, the very first interrupt would either fault while trying to push, or push the user's registers onto attacker-controlled memory. So the TSS is set up at boot, before interrupts are enabled.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. This is why a kernel needs a per-CPU TSS, not a per-process one: RSP0 is consulted on every transition into the kernel, and each CPU needs its own kernel stack to land on.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Machine-instance notes — concepts these chips introduced live in their own atomic notes; this tree records what was novel about each implementation.
Concepts in play:

html:ul.html:li: Cache hierarchy — Pentium adopted L1/L2 caching and split the L1 into separate instruction and data caches.
Branch prediction — branch history table (BHT) and branch target buffer (BTB) combined with static prediction as a fallback on BHT miss.
Speculative execution — execute past a predicted branch, write back only after the prediction is confirmed.

html:ol.html:li: Fetch / Prefetch: read from instruction cache.
Decode 1: decode to internal format; branch prediction.
Decode 2: convert complex instructions via Microarchitecture to Micro-operations.
Execute.
Write.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Back-end: two integer ALUs (also doing address calculations) and one floating-point ALU. The FP register file was arranged as a stack, with most FP ops operating on the top — zero-cycle stack swaps existed but still occupied a decode slot.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Estimated 30% of Pentium transistors went to legacy support, mostly the microcode ROM.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Concepts introduced:

html:ul.html:li: out-of-order execution — instructions execute in dataflow order, commit in program order.
Reorder buffer — 40-entry ROB tracks all in-flight instructions and assists register renaming.
Reservation station — three-instruction dispatch, up to five-issue per cycle.
CISC vs RISC decoupling — x86 in front, Micro-operations internally.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Simple and complex decoders are split because many CISC instructions translate to one or two micro-ops — the simple decoders handle those in parallel with the slow complex decoder.
Remark. 40% of Pentium Pro transistors went to backward-compatibility support.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Machine-instance notes for the PowerPC line — a RISC implementation of the same concepts covered in superscalar and Branch prediction, with a few innovations specific to this family.
Front-end. standard 4-stage pipeline. Instruction queue dispatches 3 instructions per cycle.

html:ul.html:li: Identifies unconditional branches (and resolved conditional ones) in the queue; if taken, deletes the branch instruction and starts fetching from the target — branch folding.
Untaken branches "fall out" — fall through.
No Reorder buffer-equivalent. Static scheduling.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Back-end. one integer ALU (also handles addresses), one floating-point ALU with single-cycle fused multiply-add. For FP-heavy code the integer ALU just does addresses.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Competed with two-integer-ALU designs because simpler logic left room for 4× the L1 cache (32KB vs 8KB).

html:ul.html:li: Branch dispatch on a separate bus, so branches don't compete with non-branch dispatch.
Up to 2 non-branch instructions dispatched per cycle.
Adds a commit unit and completion queue — the PowerPC names for Reorder buffer. Dynamic scheduling with an instruction window.
Two added execution units: a system unit for misc integer ops and a load/store unit. Lighter load on the integer ALU.
FPU took hits: 3 instructions every 4 cycles (mandatory bubble); double precision (including fmadd) takes 2 cycles in execute.
Splits decode/dispatch and writeback into two stages — enables shorter clock cycles.
Up to 6 instructions dispatched per cycle.
Dynamic Branch prediction with a 512-entry BHT (2 bits per entry) and a 64-entry branch address cache (BTB).
Adds a condition-register unit sharing dispatch bus with the branch unit.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Back-end. two simple integer units + one complex; fully pipelined FPU.
Dispatch obeys Reservation station rules: in-order dispatch, execution-unit availability, ROB availability, and rename-register availability.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Shallow pipeline → small ROB and few reservation stations are acceptable. Tradeoff: lower clock speed.
Remark. The 604e doubled L1 to 64KB and added a condition-register unit, freeing the integer and branch units from condition computations.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Same 4-stage pipeline as the 603e. Two integer units instead of one; 3-cycle single-precision FP; 6-entry instruction queue, up to 4 fetched per cycle.

The 750's signature trick: a branch target instruction cache (BTIC). A traditional BTB stores the address of the branch target — you still have to re-fetch that instruction. The BTIC caches the actual first instructions at the target, eliminating the re-fetch latency. This makes Branch prediction dramatically more useful.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Did not support a SIMD instruction set.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Added a vector permute unit (VPU) and vector ALU (VALU) for SIMD; full double-precision FPU; 8-entry instruction queue.
Two contrasting approaches to using a transistor budget, well-paired as instance studies because they target the same constraints from opposite directions.
Optimizes for instruction-level parallelism. Seven-stage pipeline.

html:ol.html:li: Additional second fetching stage.

Issue stage. To avoid in-order issue stalls, the G4e uses issue queues (general / vector / float) that issue 3, 2, and 1 instruction per cycle respectively. Issue queues differ from Reservation stations in that each queue feeds a group of execution units, not a single one.
Complete stage.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Optimizes for clock speed. 20-stage pipeline — extreme depth for fast clock, with the obvious downside that mispredicted branches are very expensive.

html:ul.html:li: Needs deep buffers (126-entry Reorder buffer) to keep the fast back-end fed.
x86 decoding was too slow to keep a 20-stage pipeline fed.
The trace cache moves to L1, storing already-decoded Micro-operations traces; the raw instruction cache moves to L2.
Trace cache is indexed during Branch prediction, so speculative paths past a branch can be stored in the same trace.
Operates in two modes — execution mode (trace hit, feed micro-ops directly) and build mode (trace miss, decode and assemble while feeding forward).

fr:title.text: Pentium M, Core Duo, Core 2 Duo

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Pentium M, Core Duo, Core 2 Duo

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The post-Pentium-4 generations, designed under the Power wall. Each generation contributed an innovation to the concept stack.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A processor often has three names: a brand name, a microarchitecture brand name, and a microarchitecture code name.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: First chip explicitly designed for low power. Innovations:

html:ul.html:li: Widened fetch buffer to 64 bytes — decodes up to 4 16-byte x86 instructions at a time.

Micro-op fusion — pairs of Micro-operations from the same x86 instruction (e.g. store-address + store-data, or load + ALU op for a read-modify) are tracked together as one queue entry until issue. See Micro-operations.
Loop detector in Branch prediction — counters watch loop trip counts; loops that iterate the same number of times each invocation can be predicted with 100% accuracy.
Indirect predictor — like a BHT but stores each branch's favorite target address.
Stack execution unit — dedicated unit with its own adder to handle push/pop ESP updates without going through the integer ALU. Stack micro-ops never reach the back-end.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The first true Multicore x86: two cores on the same die, sharing L2. Innovations relative to Pentium M:

html:ul.html:li: Every decoder is capable of micro-op fusion (up to 3 fused entries into the micro-op queue per cycle).
Fuses vector SSE instructions in addition to scalars.
Improved loop detector for short loops.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Until this point, the "core" was an abstraction — front-end + back-end + L1. The Core Duo is when "core" became a thing chips have more than one of.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Theme: "more is always better" — wider decode/dispatch/issue, bigger Reorder buffer, more Reservation stations, more decode logic. Two innovations made the extra resources pay off:

html:ul.html:li: Macro fusion (front-end) — fuse pairs of x86 instructions (e.g. compare + branch) before decode. Max decode bandwidth grows to 5 x86 instructions per cycle without burning ROB or RS entries.

Memory disambiguation (back-end) — apply Speculative execution to memory aliasing. Loads can speculatively execute ahead of stores with unknown target addresses; on actual alias detected at commit time, the load and dependent ops are squashed and re-executed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The point of memory disambiguation: stores must commit in program order relative to other stores, and no load can commit ahead of an aliased store. But the rule "no load past a store-data with unknown target" forced a lot of unnecessary stalls — most unknowns aren't actually aliases. Speculate and verify.
Back-end also gained single-cycle vector ops: a 128-bit data bus to the vector ALU replaced the previous 80-bit bus, so 128-bit SSE instructions decode as a single micro-op.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A load is one micro-op because address computation and memory access are inherently serial. A store is two micro-ops (store-address + store-data) because address computation and reading the register file can happen in parallel.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: n-bit computing refers to how many bits a register can hold. 64-bit means registers hold 64 bits, 64-bit instructions means their operands are 64 bits wide.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Instruction encoding size hasn't actually changed — the PC widened because the virtual address space did.
Remark. Only integer and addressing units widened. Floating point and vector units already operated on wider registers.

html:ul.html:li: Increased dynamic range — values can range over 2^{64} instead of 2^{32}. Overflow/underflow happens at the extremes of the representable range.
64-bit address space. x86-64 currently implements 40-bit virtual addresses but the architecture allows far more.
Drawback: pointers now take up twice the cache space.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. It is not always beneficial to use 64-bit integers. If you don't need the range, the wider type wastes cache space. The default integer size on x86-64 is still 32 bits; widening requires a one-byte prefix, adding less than 10% to code size.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In a Multicore system where every core has its own Cache hierarchy, the same memory address can live in multiple caches simultaneously. A cache coherence protocol is the mechanism that keeps those copies consistent — propagating writes, invalidating stale copies, and preserving two invariants:

html:ol.html:li: Single-writer invariant: at any moment, at most one core has permission to write a given line. No other core can read or write it concurrently.
Data-value invariant: all cores see the same order of updates to a given line.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two organizational families:

html:ul.html:li: snoop-based coherence — every cache watches a shared bus and reacts to other cores' traffic. Scales to ~8 cores.
directory-based coherence — a structure tracks who has a copy of each line; coherence messages are point-to-point. Scales to hundreds of cores at the cost of an extra hop.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The concrete state machines are protocols like MSI protocol, MESI protocol, MOESI protocol, and DEC Firefly, each adding states to handle more cases or to optimize specific sharing patterns. A worked implementation of directory-based MESI in Murphi lives in Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi.
Worked instance: Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi implements directory-based MESI in Murphi.
The hard limit on per-core clock speed that emerged in the early 2000s, ending the era of frequency scaling. Power dissipation has two components on a chip:

html:ul.html:li: Dynamic power density — watts per unit area dissipated by transistor switching. Scales with clock frequency: faster switching, more energy per second. You can also drive it up by cramming more transistors into the same area.
Static power density — watts per unit area dissipated by leakage current through "off" transistors. Smaller transistors leak more, so denser chips leak more even when idle.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By the mid-2000s, single-core frequency scaling was thermally unsustainable. The industry pivoted to low-power design and Multicore — get more throughput from many slower cores instead of one faster one. Pentium M and the Core series were the first generations explicitly designed under this constraint.

The network connecting cores, L2/L3 banks, and memory controllers on a Multicore chip. The same abstractions that govern off-chip networks (topology, routing, flow control) apply, but the constraints are different: wires are abundant but die area is scarce, latency is dominated by hops rather than propagation, and the network is part of the Cache coherence critical path.
The measurements used to compare network topologies and traffic patterns.

html:ul.html:li: Degree: number of links at each node. A proxy for router complexity — each port costs a buffer, crossbar input, state machine.

Bisection bandwidth: bandwidth across a cut that splits the network into two equal-sized halves. The minimum over all such cuts. A proxy for peak achievable throughput, especially for uniformly random traffic.
Diameter: maximum hop distance between any two nodes. The worst-case hop count.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Bisection bandwidth is less useful for on-chip networks than off-chip because global on-chip wiring is plentiful.
Remark. Ring bisection is 2 (two cuts to bisect); torus bisection is 4.

html:ul.html:li: Hop count: hops between two specific nodes. The average over a traffic distribution is a useful proxy for latency.
Maximum channel load: the most-loaded link's load relative to per-node injection bandwidth, for a given traffic pattern. The proxy for sustained throughput.
Path diversity: number of distinct shortest paths between two nodes. Higher path diversity allows adaptive routing to spread load.
Zero-load latency: average hop count with no contention.
Saturation throughput: the injection rate at which latency curves upward exponentially.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Hop count is not the same as latency. Per-hop pipeline depth and link propagation matter — more short hops can beat fewer slow ones. Router pipeline depth isn't always known in advance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two organizational families for on-chip networks.

html:ul.html:li: Direct — every terminal node (core, memory controller) is associated with a router. Most on-chip designs are direct because area is precious and there's no point spending a router with no compute attached. The "k-ary n-cube" family (Ring topology, Mesh topology, Torus topology) is direct.

Indirect — routers may exist that connect only to other routers, with no terminal attached. Butterfly network and Clos network are indirect; a router in their middle stages just shuffles bits between input and output stages.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Indirect networks can shorten the diameter for the same node count, but they pay for extra silicon area for routers that don't serve traffic locally. Often the right call off-chip; usually the wrong call on-chip.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The simplest indirect network: an n \times m grid of switch points connecting n inputs to m outputs. Any input can be connected to any free output simultaneously — non-blocking.

html:ul.html:li: One hop per transit (minimum possible).
Serializes if multiple senders target the same receiver.
Switch area grows as O(n \cdot m) — the dominant cost as systems grow.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Crossbar router area scales as the square of the port count, which is why high-radix routers are dramatically more expensive than low-radix ones.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Crossbars are common as the inner switch of larger structured networks (Clos network, Fat tree) — large flat crossbars are impractical at scale, but small crossbars are cheap.

A direct network where each node connects to two neighbors, forming a closed loop. Bidirectional rings allow either direction of travel; unidirectional rings allow only one. The simplest topology to implement and reason about.
In the k-ary n-cube family, a ring is a k-ary 1-cube.

html:ul.html:li: Degree 2 (every node).
Diameter \lfloor k/2 \rfloor for a bidirectional k-node ring.
Bisection bandwidth 2 — cut the ring at exactly two links.
Hardware overhead very low: routers have two ports.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Worst-case hop count is O(k). Doesn't scale beyond a small number of nodes.
Remark. Despite the poor scaling, rings are common in commercial CPUs (Intel's L3 ring bus on Sandy/Ivy Bridge) because they're cheap and 8–16 cores is enough.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A 2D grid of nodes with neighbors connected on the four compass directions, no wrap-around. The dominant on-chip topology in commercial designs (Xeon Phi, recent server x86 chips) because it lays out naturally on a 2D die.

html:ul.html:li: Interior nodes degree 4; edge nodes degree 3; corners degree 2.
Average hop count for an even-k mesh: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{3}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/4 — twice that of a Torus topology.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Non-uniform node degree. Edge nodes carry less traffic than they could, while interior nodes bottleneck on traffic flowing through them.

Remark. Mesh dominates Torus topology in practice despite the topology metrics being worse, because torus wrap-around wires are physically long and force expensive folding to equalize link lengths. Manufacturing wins over theoretical performance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Mesh topology with wrap-around links connecting the ends of each dimension. In the k-ary n-cube classification, a k-ary n-cube. Uniform node degree (every node has degree 2n), uniform load distribution, half the hop count of a mesh.

html:ul.html:li: Every node has degree 2n.
Average hop count for even k: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{4}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/8 — half that of a Mesh topology.
Good path diversity: many shortest paths between any two nodes.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Wrap-around links are physically long. To equalize wire delays, the torus must be folded, doubling effective wire lengths and link area. The wrap-around also doubles the link count versus a mesh of the same dimensions.
Remark. Despite the theoretical advantage, mesh wins on real chips. Tori show up in HPC interconnects (Cray) where the wires can run on dedicated cables.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Variants:

html:ul.html:li: k-ary n-cube: k nodes along each of n dimensions, all ends wrapped.
Asymmetric: (i, j, k)-ary — different node count per dimension.
Mesh: same as torus but without wrap-around.
Ring: k-ary 1-cube.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A multistage indirect network. A k-ary n-fly has k^n terminal nodes connected through n stages of k \times k crossbar switches. At each stage, the routing decision selects which of k sub-groups to enter; after n stages, every terminal can reach every other.

html:ul.html:li: Hop count n - 1.
Under uniform random traffic, maximum channel load is 1 (perfectly balanced).
Channel load drops further for non-uniform traffic that respects the natural partitioning.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Zero path diversity. There is exactly one path between any source and destination. A single hot link wedges traffic.
Solution. Add shuffle stages that permute paths after each routing stage, so the same source-destination pair can be reached by multiple routes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A three-stage indirect network parameterized by (m, n, r): r input switches and r output switches each of size n \times m, with m middle-stage switches each r \times r. Total terminal nodes N = nr. Non-blocking when m \geq 2n - 1.

html:ul.html:li: Number of total switching elements scales as N^{1.5} versus N^2 for a flat Crossbar while preserving non-blocking.
Path diversity is m — multiple distinct paths between any source and destination.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Proof. There are rmn input-switch elements, mr^2 middle-switch elements, and rmn output-switch elements: S = 2rmn + mr^2. With r = N/n: S = 2Nm + m(N/n)^2. Choosing n = \sqrt {N} and m \geq 2n - 1 = O(\sqrt {N}): S = 2mN + 2m\sqrt {N} = O(N^{1.5}).
Remark. A folded Clos network — input and output stages combined, making n input ports and m output ports bidirectional — is structurally the same as a Fat tree.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A tree topology with bandwidth that doubles at every level toward the root. Each interior switch has logical degree 4, but as you climb the tree, link width doubles to absorb the combined traffic of its subtree — preserving bisection bandwidth.

Structurally, a fat tree is a folded Clos network: the input and output stages of a Clos are merged into a single bidirectional stage at each level of the tree, and the middle stage becomes the higher levels of the tree.

html:ul.html:li: Each switch has logical degree 4 (2 down, 2 up — with link doubling).
Bisection bandwidth scales linearly with the number of leaves.
Path diversity grows toward the root.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Fat trees are the dominant topology for off-chip HPC interconnects (InfiniBand fat-tree clusters). Less common on-chip because the doubling links are hard to lay out on a 2D die.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given a topology and a packet's source and destination, which path through the network does it take? Three classes of answer:

html:ul.html:li:
Greedy (shortest-path) — always pick the shortest available route.
(+) Minimizes latency under light load.
(-) Overloads central links; worst-case throughput poor.

Uniform / oblivious random — pick a direction at random.
(+) Spreads load; avoids systematic hotspots.
(-) Wastes bandwidth on non-minimal paths; latency unpredictable.

Adaptive — at each hop, pick the outgoing link with the lowest local congestion.
(+) Dynamically balances load; best worst-case throughput.
(-) Routers need congestion state; risk of livelock without care.

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Example. Tornado traffic on an 8-node bidirectional ring: node i sends to (i+3) \bmod 8.
Greedy: all packets travel counter-clockwise. Bidirectional bandwidth wasted; \frac {1}{3} utilization.
Uniform: randomly clockwise/counter-clockwise. Counter-clockwise gets \frac {2}{3} utilization, clockwise \frac {2}{5}; worst-case \frac {2}{5}.
Adaptive: sends \frac {5}{8} counter-clockwise to match traffic exactly. Best.
Remark. Adaptive routing tends to need Network backpressure signaling to know where congestion is, and care to avoid livelock when many adaptive decisions chase each other.

Remark. Oblivious strategies like Dimension-order routing and Valiant's routing algorithm give deterministic guarantees (deadlock-free, bounded path length) at the cost of giving up the load-balancing of true adaptive routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An oblivious Network routing strategies for k-ary n-cube networks (Mesh topology, Torus topology): always exhaust the offset in one dimension completely before changing to the next. E.g. in 2D mesh routing, route X-then-Y — never turn back to X after starting Y.

The dimension order prevents routing deadlock: cycles in the channel dependency graph are impossible because no packet ever requests a channel in a "lower" dimension once it has started using a higher one. The dependency graph is therefore acyclic, and the standard cycle-equals-deadlock argument applies.

html:ul.html:li: (+) Provably deadlock-free without virtual channels.
(-) Zero path diversity. Hot spots can't be routed around.
(-) Unaware of current network state — oblivious.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The standard remedy for the path-diversity problem is Valiant's routing algorithm, which keeps the deadlock-freedom of dimension-order routing while spreading load with a randomized intermediate hop.
An oblivious Network routing strategies that achieves provably optimal worst-case throughput by introducing randomness in path selection:

html:ol.html:li: For a packet going from s to d, randomly pick an intermediate node w.
Route s \to w using Dimension-order routing.
Eject the packet at w, then re-inject it.
Route w \to d using Dimension-order routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To preserve deadlock-freedom, the intermediate node must remove the packet from the network before re-injecting — otherwise the channel dependencies of the two halves could interlock.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Restricting w to lie in the sub-quadrant defined by s and d keeps paths near-minimal while still spreading load — the practical compromise between guaranteed throughput and added latency.

Remark. Valiant's algorithm trades average-case latency (paths are longer) for worst-case throughput (any traffic pattern looks roughly uniform once randomized). The right call for adversarial workloads; overkill for uniform traffic.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Routing deadlock occurs when a packet cannot make progress because the buffer at its next link is full, and the packet holding that buffer is itself blocked waiting on a buffer this packet holds — a cycle in the channel dependency graph.
Two structural responses:

html:ol.html:li: Avoid deadlock by construction. Dimension-order routing enforces an ordering on dimension transitions so the dependency graph is acyclic by design.

Detect and recover from deadlock. Allow all turns. Detect cycles via timeouts. Drain stuck packets onto a backup network (a "slow channel" — physical, or more commonly an escape virtual channel) that uses dimension-order routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The relationship to other related concepts:

html:ul.html:li: Backpressure (Network backpressure) is the signaling mechanism that lets a router downstream tell upstream it is congested, before the upstream actually fills the link.
Adaptive routing avoids deadlock locally; backpressure lets adaptive routing react to remote congestion.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A signaling mechanism that lets a downstream router tell upstream "I am congested — slow down" before its buffers actually overflow. With backpressure information, adaptive routing can route around remote congestion, not just local.

The mechanism propagates: a congested link signals back to its sender, which then knows its next link will fill, which signals further back. Useful information flows in the opposite direction of the data.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Acting only on local state can cause livelock — packets bounce between hotspots, never converging.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Backpressure breaks the livelock by giving local routers global-ish information without an actual global view.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Backpressure is mechanism; adaptive routing is policy. The two together are what real high-radix on-chip networks use.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The on-chip network connects each core (with its L1) and the L2 cache banks (which may be shared or private). Each L2 bank has a cache controller (manages Cache coherence) and, at memory boundaries, a memory controller (talks to DRAM).
Two cache-organization choices interact with the network design:

html:ul.html:li: Private L2 caches: each tile owns its L2 slice; cross-tile traffic happens only on private-L2 miss. (+) Lower hit latency; less interconnect traffic. (-) Replicated data; more off-chip traffic on misses.
Shared L2 cache: a single logical L2 split into banks, with each address mapped to a home bank. (+) Better utilization, no replication. (-) Every L1 miss goes on the network.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The interface to the network sits at the cache controller:

html:ul.html:li: Miss status handling register (MSHR): on a cache miss, records what request the cache is waiting on. Matched against the reply when it arrives.
Transaction status handling register (TSHR): tracks outstanding memory transactions to off-chip memory from the directory cache.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Memory controllers are commonly placed at the chip perimeter, near the I/O pads. A memory controller can share a router with a core/cache tile (saves area; needs arbitration) or have its own (more area; no contention).
Remark. Hardware-managed shared memory is one design point; another is explicit message passing, where software (not hardware) is responsible for moving data between processes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Links. Thicker wires with larger spacing → lower resistance, higher speed, better energy efficiency. The wire budget determines how many ports you can afford per router.

Routers. Each port costs a buffer, a crossbar input, state logic, and a link to its neighbor. Crossbar area scales as the square of port count, so high-radix routers are dramatically expensive. Rings have very simple routers but poor performance.

Floorplan. Tori need to be folded to equalize wire lengths, doubling effective wire length; meshes don't. Tori also have twice as many links as meshes, which may force narrower links and lower per-link bandwidth.
Folding can also equalize path lengths inside a network at the cost of increasing average latency.
Concentration allocates more cores per router. (-) Decreases throughput per node. (+) Programs with high locality do little remote communication, so concentration is a good fit there.
General topologies aren't always optimal. Two construction strategies:

html:ul.html:li: Splitting: start with a large crossbar; split into smaller switches until constraints are met.
Merging: start with many small switches and combine.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Hierarchical topologies use one topology within clusters and another across clusters — common when traffic has locality.

fr:title.text: Pentium M, Core Duo, Core 2 Duo

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Pentium M, Core Duo, Core 2 Duo

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The post-Pentium-4 generations, designed under the Power wall. Each generation contributed an innovation to the concept stack.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A processor often has three names: a brand name, a microarchitecture brand name, and a microarchitecture code name.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: First chip explicitly designed for low power. Innovations:

html:ul.html:li: Widened fetch buffer to 64 bytes — decodes up to 4 16-byte x86 instructions at a time.

Micro-op fusion — pairs of Micro-operations from the same x86 instruction (e.g. store-address + store-data, or load + ALU op for a read-modify) are tracked together as one queue entry until issue. See Micro-operations.
Loop detector in Branch prediction — counters watch loop trip counts; loops that iterate the same number of times each invocation can be predicted with 100% accuracy.
Indirect predictor — like a BHT but stores each branch's favorite target address.
Stack execution unit — dedicated unit with its own adder to handle push/pop ESP updates without going through the integer ALU. Stack micro-ops never reach the back-end.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The first true Multicore x86: two cores on the same die, sharing L2. Innovations relative to Pentium M:

html:ul.html:li: Every decoder is capable of micro-op fusion (up to 3 fused entries into the micro-op queue per cycle).
Fuses vector SSE instructions in addition to scalars.
Improved loop detector for short loops.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Until this point, the "core" was an abstraction — front-end + back-end + L1. The Core Duo is when "core" became a thing chips have more than one of.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Theme: "more is always better" — wider decode/dispatch/issue, bigger Reorder buffer, more Reservation stations, more decode logic. Two innovations made the extra resources pay off:

html:ul.html:li: Macro fusion (front-end) — fuse pairs of x86 instructions (e.g. compare + branch) before decode. Max decode bandwidth grows to 5 x86 instructions per cycle without burning ROB or RS entries.

Memory disambiguation (back-end) — apply Speculative execution to memory aliasing. Loads can speculatively execute ahead of stores with unknown target addresses; on actual alias detected at commit time, the load and dependent ops are squashed and re-executed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The point of memory disambiguation: stores must commit in program order relative to other stores, and no load can commit ahead of an aliased store. But the rule "no load past a store-data with unknown target" forced a lot of unnecessary stalls — most unknowns aren't actually aliases. Speculate and verify.
Back-end also gained single-cycle vector ops: a 128-bit data bus to the vector ALU replaced the previous 80-bit bus, so 128-bit SSE instructions decode as a single micro-op.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A load is one micro-op because address computation and memory access are inherently serial. A store is two micro-ops (store-address + store-data) because address computation and reading the register file can happen in parallel.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The hard limit on per-core clock speed that emerged in the early 2000s, ending the era of frequency scaling. Power dissipation has two components on a chip:

html:ul.html:li: Dynamic power density — watts per unit area dissipated by transistor switching. Scales with clock frequency: faster switching, more energy per second. You can also drive it up by cramming more transistors into the same area.
Static power density — watts per unit area dissipated by leakage current through "off" transistors. Smaller transistors leak more, so denser chips leak more even when idle.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By the mid-2000s, single-core frequency scaling was thermally unsustainable. The industry pivoted to low-power design and Multicore — get more throughput from many slower cores instead of one faster one. Pentium M and the Core series were the first generations explicitly designed under this constraint.

Multiple tiers of progressively-larger, progressively-slower storage between the CPU's registers and main memory. Each tier holds the most-recently-used contents of the next slower tier, exploiting Spatial locality and Temporal locality in the workload.
Approximate tiers on a modern x86 chip:

html:ul.html:li: Registers — ns, dozens of bytes per core.
L1 cache — 2–8 ns, 8–128 KB per core. Often split into instruction and data L1s.
Built from static RAM (4–6 transistors per cell), which is fast but area-expensive vs. DRAM's single-transistor cell.
L2 cache — 5–12 ns, 0.5–8 MB. Closes the latency gap between L1 and DRAM.
L3 cache — slower, shared across cores.
Main memory — 10–60 ns, GBs.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. A byte's journey. L1 checked first; on miss, L2; on miss, main memory; on miss in main memory, Page fault to disk.

Remark. L1 is split into instruction and data caches because the locality profiles of code and data are very different — re-running the same code over different data has high temporal locality for code but low for data.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The functional circuitry that actually performs an instruction. A superscalar CPU contains several, dispatched to in parallel during the execute stage of the pipeline. The common families:

html:ul.html:li: ALU — arithmetic logic unit, performs arithmetic (add, sub, mul, div) and logical (AND, OR, NOT) operations on integers and on special registers like the PSW. Typically split into integer logic units (ILU) and floating-point units (FPU).
Load-store unit — performs memory loads and stores. Carries a small address-calculation adder for address-mode resolution.
Branch execution unit — evaluates branch conditions (often by reading the PSW) and signals whether the branch is taken.
Vector / SIMD units — operate on wide registers (SSE, AVX, AltiVec) for data-parallel work.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Per the convention in Inside the Machine, "ALU" often refers to any execution unit for arithmetic or logical operations — including integer logic units (ILU) and floating-point units (FPU).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each unit's reads and writes flow through a Register file. Splitting the register file per-unit reduces port count and access time.
When the correctness of a program depends on the order in which concurrent Threads schedule, you have a race condition. The classic example: two threads each execute
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: mov addr reg // load add 1 reg // increment mov reg addr // store
fr:mainmatter.html:p: intending to increment a shared variable twice. If the OS interleaves the threads at the wrong instruction boundary:

html:ol.html:li: Thread 1 stores its incremented value.
Thread 2 stores its incremented value.
Thread 1 increments (in its register).
Thread 2 increments (in its register).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The result is a single increment, not two — both threads loaded the same starting value and the second store clobbered the first.
The fix is to prevent interleaving inside the code region that touches shared state — the Critical section — using a synchronization primitive like Locks.

A lock is a synchronization primitive that grants mutually exclusive access to a critical section. The implementations below trade off coherence traffic, fairness, and memory in different ways. The naive locks (Test-and-set lock and Test-and-test-and-set lock) are simple but generate O(n) coherence traffic per release; queue-based locks (Ticket lock, Array-based lock, MCS lock) scale better at the cost of extra state per waiter.

The simplest spin lock. The acquire loop spins on an atomic test_and_set: it writes LOCKED and reads the previous value in one step. If the previous value was UNLOCKED, the caller has the lock; otherwise it tries again.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Major coherence traffic. Every spinning core must acquire write permissions and invalidate everyone else on each test_and_set, so contention scales badly with the number of waiters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A refinement of the Test-and-set lock that reduces coherence traffic during the spin. Threads first perform a plain load to check whether the lock looks free, and only attempt the atomic test_and_set when it does. Spinning is then read-only, so cores can share the cache line.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Spinning is a read-only operation now.

Problem. When the lock is released, the line must be invalidated on every waiter, and they all simultaneously read and try to write. The contention is shifted from steady state to release time but is still O(n) in the number of waiters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A fair spin lock based on two counters. Each arriving thread takes a "ticket" by atomically incrementing next_ticket and then spins until now_serving equals its ticket.
